perturbation.original_text: "Franchisee" - The party to whom the Franchise is granted by the Franchisor, Pretzel Time, Inc. The term is applicable to one or more persons, a corporation or a partnership, as the case may be. If two or more persons are at any time the Franchisee hereunder, their obligations and liabilities to Pretzel Time shall be joint and several. References to Franchisee and assignee which are applicable to an individual or individuals shall mean the Owner (defined below) or Principal Owners (defined below) of the equity or operating control of the Franchisee or the assignee, if the Franchisee or the assignee is a corporation or partnership.
perturbation.changed_text: "Franchisee" - The party to whom the Franchise is granted by the Franchisor, Pretzel Time, Inc. The term is applicable to one or more persons, a corporation or a partnership, as the case may be. If two or more persons are at any time the Franchisee hereunder, their obligations and liabilities to Pretzel Time shall be individual and not joint. References to Franchisee and assignee which are applicable to an individual or individuals shall mean the Owner (defined below) or Principal Owners (defined below) of the equity or operating control of the Franchisee or the assignee, if the Franchisee or the assignee is a corporation or partnership.
perturbation.explanation: By changing the term 'joint and several' to 'individual and not joint', the contractual language directly contradicts the standard legal interpretation of obligations when multiple parties are involved. This creates uncertainty about the extent of liability for each franchisee if there are multiple franchisees.
perturbation.law_explanation: UPA § 306 states that all partners are liable jointly and severally for all obligations of the partnership. By stating their obligations are individual and not joint, it goes against the definition in the UPA and creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.justification: The original contract states obligations are 'joint and several,' while the changed text says obligations are 'individual and not joint.' The Law Explanation states that UPA § 306 specifies partners are liable 'jointly and severally.' The changed text directly contradicts this legal standard, as it removes joint liability, thus undermining the legal obligation. The scraped snippet from the Uniform Law Commission provides context about the existence of uniform laws, but does not contain information about joint and several liability.

perturbation.original_text: Franchisee agrees to open the Unit for business on or before the opening date specified in the lease if it has the Landlord's approval and only after Pretzel Time notifies Franchisee that the conditions set forth in Sections 8 and 9 have been satisfied.
perturbation.changed_text: Franchisee agrees to open the Unit for business on or before the opening date specified in the lease regardless of the Landlord's approval or notification from Pretzel Time that the conditions set forth in Sections 8 and 9 have been satisfied.
perturbation.explanation: This change implies that Franchisee can ignore safety and regulatory prerequisites set by Pretzel Time and the landlord, introducing ambiguity as to whether the unit can function without those prerequisites or if that opens up liability.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Occupational Safety and Health Act of 1970 § 5(a)(1)
perturbation.law_explanation: This contradicts the OSH Act's General Duty Clause, which requires employers to furnish a place of employment free from recognized hazards. By opening regardless of landlord approval or fulfilled prerequisites, the franchisee could be in violation of regulations.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Duties | Occupational Safety and Health Administration Skip to main content SEC. 5. Duties (a) Each employer -- (1) More... 29 USC 654 shall furnish to each of his employees employment and a place of employment which are free from recognized hazards that are causing or are likely to cause death or serious physical harm to his employees; (2) shall comply with occupational safety and health standards promulgated under this Act. (b) Each employee shall comply with occupational safety and health standards and all rules, regulations, and orders issued pursuant to this Act which are applicable to his own actions and conduct.
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the OSH Act's General Duty Clause, as described in the Law Explanation. The original contract requires franchisee to wait for landlord approval and Pretzel Time's notification that safety conditions are met. The changed text removes these prerequisites, explicitly stating the franchisee can open 'regardless of the Landlord's approval or notification from Pretzel Time'. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states employers 'shall furnish to each of his employees employment and a place of employment which are free from recognized hazards that are causing or are likely to cause death or serious physical harm to his employees'. By removing the requirement for safety checks and landlord approval, the franchisee risks creating a hazardous environment for employees, directly contravening the employer's duty under the OSH Act.

perturbation.original_text: To compensate Pretzel Time for the increased administrative expense of handling late payments, Pretzel Time may charge Franchisee a $50.00 late charge for each delinquent payment.
perturbation.changed_text: To compensate Pretzel Time for the increased administrative expense of handling late payments, Pretzel Time must charge Franchisee a $50.00 late charge for each delinquent payment, irrespective of actual expenses.
perturbation.explanation: By changing “may charge” to “must charge” and adding 'irrespective of actual expenses' this clause now operates as a penalty rather than a valid liquidated damage. This changes how the late fee may be considered under law.
perturbation.law_explanation: The clause now risks being considered a penalty which is generally unenforceable under contract law. Liquidated damages are only acceptable to the degree they represent an honest and good-faith attempt to estimate the prospective damages, which is put into question with the phrase 'irrespective of actual expenses'

perturbation.justification: The changed text transforms a discretionary late fee ("may charge") into a mandatory one ("must charge, irrespective of actual expenses"). The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both state that this makes the charge function as a penalty rather than valid liquidated damages. The addition of 'irrespective of actual expenses' removes the link between the charge and actual costs, supporting the 'penalty' designation.
